title: Postgres Performance Migration for Vector Backend
topic: Postgres performance migration for Vector backend — what's needed to adopt indexes, PgBouncer (transaction mode), materialised views, JSONB GIN, and slow-query analysis on the existing pgxpool stack: impact on Go code (internal/db/db.go pgx prepared-statement cache trap), migration sequencing, deployment infra, monitoring, and rollback. Frame as 'from current direct pgxpool → fully tuned Postgres' with ranked phases.
summary: Five-phase plan from current pgxpool stack to fully tuned Postgres — diagnose, index, JSONB GIN, mat views, PgBouncer.
content:
Contents
  1. 1. Overview
  2. 2. Current Vector Stack (Baseline)
  3. 3. Phase Map
  4. 4. Phase 1 — Diagnose
  5. 5. Phase 2 — Indexes
  6. 6. Phase 3 — JSONB GIN
  7. 7. Phase 4 — Materialised Views
  8. 8. Phase 5 — PgBouncer
  9. 9. Risks & Rollback
  10. 10. Sources
  11. 11. Gaps

1. Overview

This paper maps the work needed to migrate the Vector backend from its current direct pgxpool Postgres connection to a fully tuned production stack. Application Go code is untouched in 4 of the 5 phases — most of the migration is additive SQL, additive infrastructure, and observability-only changes. The one exception is PgBouncer, which has a well-known interaction with pgx's prepared-statement cache that must be handled in backend/internal/db/db.go.

The phases are ranked by impact-per-risk, lowest-risk first. The recommended sequence diagnoses before changing anything (Phase 1), commits SQL-only additions (Phases 2, 3, 4), and finally takes the one infra step that touches Go code (Phase 5).

2. Current Vector Stack (Baseline)

ComponentCurrent state
Driverjackc/pgx/v5 via pgxpool
Pool configMinConns=2, MaxConnIdleTime=5m, default extended-protocol query mode (prepared-statement cache on)
ConnectionDirect to Postgres on localhost:5435 (dev tunnel) — no pooler in front
Migration systemFile-based ordered SQL under db/schema/NNN_*.sql, runner at backend/cmd/migrate
Existing indexes~292 CREATE INDEX lines across 70 migrations — pattern is well-established
Realtime substratePostgres LISTEN/NOTIFY already wired for ranking — substrate exists for materialised-view refresh triggers
DiagnosticsNeither pg_stat_statements nor auto_explain currently loaded — slow queries are invisible in aggregate

3. Phase Map

PhaseLeverCode touchedRiskReversible?
1Diagnose (pg_stat_statements, auto_explain)None — config onlylowYes — restart
2Indexes (composite + partial, CONCURRENTLY)Migration files onlylowYes — DROP INDEX
3JSONB GIN (jsonb_path_ops)Migration files onlylowYes — DROP INDEX
4Materialised views (NOTIFY-driven refresh)1 migration + 1–2 service files per viewmediumMostly — revert read path, drop view
5PgBouncer transaction-mode pooler2 lines in internal/db/db.go + DSN env vars + new infra processmediumYes — DSN flip back, restart

Recommended order: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5. Phase 1 is the prerequisite for choosing the right indexes in Phase 2 — resist skipping it.

4. Phase 1 — Diagnose: pg_stat_statements + auto_explain

The fastest way to find slow PostgreSQL queries is a combination of pg_stat_statements for historical aggregates, EXPLAIN ANALYZE for one query's real behaviour, and pg_stat_activity or logs for what is happening right now. auto_explain automatically logs execution plans of slow statements without having to run EXPLAIN by hand — especially helpful for tracking down un-optimised queries in large applications.

What to enable

  • shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_stat_statements,auto_explain' — both load at server start. Restart required.
  • pg_stat_statements.max = 10000 — covers most app query diversity (5,000–10,000 is the documented sweet spot).
  • pg_stat_statements.track = top — only top-level statements (skip statements inside functions).
  • auto_explain.log_min_duration = 200ms — start conservative; tighten later.
  • auto_explain.log_analyze = on, auto_explain.log_buffers = on.
  • auto_explain.log_timing = off at first — per-plan-node timing has heavy overhead and is rarely needed for triage.
  • auto_explain.sample_rate = 1.0 in dev / 0.1 in production — sampling reduces overhead under load.

Codebase impact

None. No Go code changes; no migrations. Configuration lives in postgresql.conf (or per-env config). Reset stats periodically with SELECT pg_stat_statements_reset(); consider scheduled resets during low-traffic periods.

Why this is Phase 1

Without pg_stat_statements, Phase 2 (indexes) is guesswork. The known 2-second client-side sort on /work-items is the canary — there are almost certainly other bottlenecks no-one has noticed. Spend a week of normal usage with diagnostics on, then index against real evidence.

5. Phase 2 — Indexes: composite + partial + CONCURRENTLY

What to add

Multi-column (composite) indexes optimise queries that filter or sort by more than one column. In a published 2026 benchmark, a composite index reduced a query on a 10-million-row table from 1.8s to 0.5s. Multi-column index ordering matters: in a composite B-tree the leftmost column is the primary sort key, so (workspace_id, status, priority) is useful for queries on workspace_id alone, on workspace_id + status, and on all three — but not for queries on priority alone.

Partial indexes use a WHERE clause to index only a subset of rows — ideal for soft-archive patterns like WHERE archived_at IS NULL. Vector's tables already use soft-archive throughout, so partial indexes are a natural fit and reduce index size + maintenance overhead substantially.

Production rule

Always use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY in production to avoid blocking writes. Postgres rejects CONCURRENTLY inside a transaction block — the migration runner needs a small change to allow concurrent index creation outside of transactions.

Codebase impact

  • SQL migrations only. New files under db/schema/NNN_*.sql, following the existing 292-index pattern.
  • One small migration-runner adjustment if the runner currently wraps every migration in a transaction — needed to support CONCURRENTLY.
  • No application code touched.

Candidate indexes (drive from Phase 1 evidence)

TableSuggested indexRationale
work_items(workspace_id, status, priority, created_at) WHERE archived_at IS NULLServer-driven sort/filter (PLA-0021 Wave 2)
addressables(viewport, kind, name) WHERE archived_at IS NULLAddress resolution hot path
audit_log(actor_id, occurred_at DESC)Per-user audit lookups
sessions(user_id) WHERE revoked = falseRefresh-token rotation hot path

These are illustrative — the real list comes from Phase 1's slowest top-50.

6. Phase 3 — JSONB GIN (jsonb_path_ops)

Vector stores rich JSON in several tables (page_help, addressables, custom_fields, error_events). If queries do containment lookups (field @> '{...}'::jsonb) without GIN support, Postgres falls back to sequential scans.

jsonb_path_ops vs jsonb_ops

ClassOperators supportedIndex sizeWhen to use
jsonb_ops (default)?, ?&, ?|, @>LargerMixed key-existence + containment queries
jsonb_path_ops@> onlySubstantially smaller, fasterContainment-only workloads (most app patterns)

In published benchmarks, jsonb_path_ops increased load time by only 16% versus 79% for jsonb_ops on bulk insert — and the resulting index was an order of magnitude smaller than the equivalent B-tree (one source measured 2.14 MB GIN vs 78.31 MB B-tree). Default to jsonb_path_ops unless you actually need the ? family of operators.

Codebase impact

  • Migrations only. Same additive pattern as Phase 2.
  • Watch DML overhead — GIN updates apply in chunks and can degrade write throughput on hot jsonb columns. Profile in dev before rolling to a hot prod table.

7. Phase 4 — Materialised Views

Materialised views cache expensive aggregate queries (portfolio rollups, work-item counts per workspace, addressable usage stats). PostgreSQL does not handle incremental updates out of the box — a single underlying row change recomputes the entire view via REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW.

Three refresh strategies

StrategyCostFreshnessVector fit
Scheduled REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLYO(total view) per refreshLag = schedule intervalGood for nightly rollups
NOTIFY-driven refreshO(total view) per change burstNear-realtime (debounced)Reuses existing LISTEN/NOTIFY substrate
pg_ivm (Incremental View Maintenance)O(delta) per changeSynchronous, in-transactionBest long-term, but adds a Postgres extension dependency

REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY requires a unique index on the view and computes new rows in a temp table before swapping in — always prefer CONCURRENTLY in production so reads stay live.

Recommended approach for Vector

  1. Start with NOTIFY-driven refresh — Vector already uses LISTEN/NOTIFY for ranking realtime, so the wiring pattern is familiar.
  2. Debounce refresh calls (250ms — same window the ranking layer uses).
  3. Switch to pg_ivm only if the O(total) cost of full refresh becomes the bottleneck.

Codebase impact

  • Per view: one migration creating the view + unique index + AFTER INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE triggers that NOTIFY on the underlying table.
  • One Go listener in the existing realtime/listener.go that subscribes to the channel and debounces a REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY call.
  • Per view: 1–2 service files change their read path from base tables to the view (writes still go to base tables — sole-writer rule preserved).
  • Lint / canary test to assert no writer service ever writes to a materialised view.

8. Phase 5 — PgBouncer (the prepared-statement trap)

PgBouncer in transaction mode multiplexes many short-lived client connections onto a smaller pool of long-lived Postgres connections. It's the single biggest infra-only lever for handling external API consumers (post-PLA-0019). The catch: pgx's default extended-protocol query mode automatically prepares and caches statements on the server, but in transaction-pool mode each transaction may land on a different server connection — so the cached statement name is invalid and queries fail.

Two solutions

Solution A — disable pgx prepared-statement cache

Two-line change in backend/internal/db/db.go:

cfg.ConnConfig.DefaultQueryExecMode = pgx.QueryExecModeExec
cfg.ConnConfig.StatementCacheCapacity = 0

Pros: works against any PgBouncer version, including older lines deployed by managed Postgres providers. Cons: gives up the per-statement parse-cache speedup pgx normally provides.

Solution B — PgBouncer 1.21+ with native prepared-statement support

PgBouncer 1.21 (release-titled "the one with prepared statements") added native protocol-level prepared-statement support in transaction mode. Set max_prepared_statements = 100 in pgbouncer.ini (10 is a reasonable starting point, 100 covers most real workloads) and pgx's cache works without modification. Synthetic benchmarks show 15–250% query throughput improvement vs Solution A.

Choicepgx changesPgBouncer versionThroughput
A — disable cache2 lines in internal/db/db.goAnyBaseline
B — enable in PgBouncerNone≥ 1.21+15–250%

Recommendation: Solution B if you control the PgBouncer install. Solution A as the safe-default fallback if you depend on a managed PgBouncer that may be on an older line.

Codebase impact

  • Connection-string change in backend/.env.dev, .env.staging, .env.production — point at the PgBouncer port instead of Postgres directly.
  • Solution A only: two-line edit in backend/internal/db/db.go.
  • Infra: one PgBouncer process per environment, supervised. Single-binary deploy.
  • Avoid double-pooling pitfalls — when running PgBouncer + pgxpool, set pgxpool's MaxConns below PgBouncer's default_pool_size so the pool never starves at the bouncer; transactions across pgxpool that span multiple PgBouncer round-trips can also see surprising state if not explicitly managed.

9. Risks & Rollback

PhaseRiskRollback
1auto_explain with log_timing=on can slow every query — do not enable in prod.Restart with libraries removed from shared_preload_libraries.
2Index bloat / write amplification on hot tables; wrong column order = unused index.DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY — instant.
3GIN updates apply in chunks; intensive DML can stall on hot jsonb columns.DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY.
4Refresh storms if NOTIFY isn't debounced; staleness window if listener crashes.Revert read path to base table; drop view.
5Forgetting to disable pgx prepared-statement cache breaks everything the moment traffic hits the pooler.Flip DSN back to direct Postgres; restart Go.

Monitoring to add

  • pg_stat_statements dashboards — top-50 by total_exec_time and by mean_exec_time.
  • Index usage — pg_stat_user_indexes.idx_scan to detect dead indexes.
  • Materialised view freshness — last-refresh timestamp per view, exposed via a status endpoint.
  • PgBouncer pool stats — SHOW POOLS; wait time, active vs waiting clients.

10. Sources

  1. Prepared Statements in Transaction Mode for PgBouncer — Crunchy Data
  2. pgx v5 package documentation — pkg.go.dev
  3. PgBouncer and pgxpool — pgx issue #970
  4. PgBouncer 1.21 adds prepared statement support in transaction mode — pganalyze
  5. Boosting Postgres Performance with Prepared Statements and PgBouncer's Transaction Mode — Tiger Data
  6. PgBouncer 1.21.0 release — "the one with prepared statements"
  7. PostgreSQL Performance Tuning Checklist: 2026 Complete Guide — DEV Community
  8. Optimizing Database Indexes — Tiger Data
  9. PostgreSQL Indexing Playbook (Feb 12, 2026) — Sachith Dassanayake
  10. PostgreSQL Documentation — Chapter 14: Performance Tips
  11. PostgreSQL Documentation — auto_explain
  12. 3 ways to detect slow queries in PostgreSQL — CYBERTEC
  13. Identify PostgreSQL Performance Bottlenecks With pg_stat_statements — Tiger Data
  14. Incremental View Maintenance — PostgreSQL wiki
  15. pg_ivm — Incremental View Maintenance extension
  16. PostgreSQL Documentation — REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW
  17. Postgres Materialized View Auto Refresh — Epsio
  18. Understanding Postgres GIN Indexes — pganalyze
  19. PostgreSQL JSONB Operator Classes of GIN Indexes and Their Usage
  20. PostgreSQL Documentation — GIN Indexes

11. Gaps

  • No empirical baseline. Phase 1 has not run yet — concrete index/view recommendations in this paper are illustrative, not evidence-based. The first action item is enabling pg_stat_statements + auto_explain for one week of normal usage.
  • Vector's PgBouncer target version is undecided. Solution A (disable pgx cache) is safe everywhere; Solution B (1.21+) requires confirming the deploy target supports it.
  • NOTIFY-driven materialised-view refresh is a Vector-specific composition pattern — it is not directly documented in upstream sources. The pattern combines well-documented pieces (LISTEN/NOTIFY + REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY) but its long-tail behaviour under refresh storms needs a dev-environment soak test before production.
  • Migration runner CONCURRENTLY support — depends on whether backend/cmd/migrate wraps each file in a transaction. Needs a quick code check before Phase 2.
